response_text:
**Give Buses the Road Space They Deserve**

On any congested urban corridor, the same scene repeats every morning and evening: a long line of cars, a bus trapped among them, and dozens of people on that bus losing time because the street has been designed to prioritize the least efficient way to move people. If cities are serious about reducing congestion, improving air quality, and making transportation fairer, they should install dedicated bus lanes on their busiest corridors.

This is not a radical idea. It is a practical one.

A single bus can carry far more people than a line of private cars occupying the same amount of street space. Yet on many major roads, buses are forced to crawl behind traffic generated largely by single-occupancy vehicles. That makes little sense. When road space is scarce, it should be allocated to the mode that moves the most people most efficiently. Dedicated bus lanes do exactly that.

The strongest argument for bus lanes is simple: they make transit faster and more reliable. Speed matters, but reliability may matter even more. A commuter can plan around a 20-minute trip. What they cannot plan around is a trip that takes 20 minutes one day and 45 the next. When buses have their own lane, they avoid the stop-and-go chaos of mixed traffic. Riders arrive on time more often, transit agencies can run service more predictably, and the bus becomes a more attractive option for everyone, including people who currently drive.

That last point is crucial. Opponents often claim that taking a lane away from cars will worsen congestion. In the short term, traffic patterns do change, and drivers may feel the impact. But the broader goal is not to make driving equally convenient forever. It is to move more people through a crowded corridor with limited space. If bus service becomes meaningfully better, some drivers will switch. Over time, that shift can reduce pressure on the corridor as a whole. Cities cannot build their way out of congestion by endlessly accommodating more cars. They can, however, offer a better alternative.

Dedicated bus lanes also make cities more equitable. Bus riders are disproportionately working people, lower-income residents, students, seniors, and people with disabilities—those who are least likely to have the flexibility or resources to absorb
